coredata.dc:title: A quadruplet electrochemical platform for ultrasensitive and simultaneous detection of ascorbic acid, dopamine, uric acid and acetaminophen based on a ferrocene derivative functional Au NPs/carbon dots nanocomposite and graphene
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Liuqing
Huang, Na
Lu, Qiujun
Liu, Meiling
Li, Haitao
Zhang, Youyu
Yao, Shouzhuo
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a new nanomaterial of thiol functional ferrocene derivative (Fc-SH) stabilized Au NPs/carbon dots nanocomposite (Au/C NC) coupling with graphene modified glassy carbon electrode (Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene/GCE) was fabricated to serve as a quadruplet detection platform for ultrasensitive and simultaneous determination of ascorbic acid (AA), dopamine (DA), uric acid (UA) and acetaminophen (AC). The Au/C NC was synthesized by adding HAuCl4 into carbon nanodots solution without using any additional reductant and stabilizing agent. Then the Fc-SH was utilized as the protective and capping agent to modify the Au/C NC. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), UV–Vis, Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), cyclic voltammetry (CV), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were adopted to characterize the morphology and electrochemical properties of the materials and the electrodes. The Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene/GCE exhibits a synergistic catalytic and amplification effects towards oxidation of AA, DA, UA and AC owing to the existence of the nanomaterial and electron mediator. When simultaneous detection of AA, DA, UA and AC, the oxidation peak potentials of the four compounds on the electrode can be well separated and the peak currents were linearly dependent on their concentrations. The quadruplet detection platform shows excellent linear range and ultrasensitive response to the four components, the detection limits were estimated to be as low as 1.00, 0.05, 0.12 and 0.10 μM (S/N = 3), and the modified electrode exhibits excellent stability and reproducibility. The proposed electrode has been successfully applied to detect of these four analytes in real samples with satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Simultaneous determination
Au/C NC
Graphene
Quadruplet detection
Signal amplification

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of Au/C NC and the modified electrodes
Electrochemical behaviors of AA, DA, UA and AC on modified electrodes
Optimization of volume ratio of Fc-S-Au/C NC to graphene
Effect of amount of Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene modified on GCE
Simultaneous detection of AA, DA, UA and AC

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of C-dots (A), Au/C NC (B), Fc-S-Au/C NC (C) and Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene (D). SEM picture (E) and EDS (F) of Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene.
UV (A) and FTIR (B) spectra of Fc-SAc (a), C-dots (b), Au/C NC (c) and Fc-S-Au/C NC (d).
CVs (A) and EIS (B) of bare GCE (a), Au/C NC (b), graphene/GCE (c), Fc-S-Au/C NC/GCE (d) and Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene/GCE (e) in [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−.

CVs of bare GCE (a), Au/C NC (b), Fc-S-Au/C NC (c), graphene/GCE (d) and Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene/GCE (e) in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4) containing 0.5 mM AA (A), 0.5 mM DA (B), 0.5 mM UA (C), 0.5 mM AC (D). Scan rate: 50 mV/s.

CVs (A) and DPVs (B) of bare GCE (a), Au/C NC (b), Fc-S-Au/C NC (c), graphene/GCE (d) and Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene/GCE (e) in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4) coexist of 1.40 mM AA, 0.03 mM DA, 0.20 mM UA, 0.06 mM AC. Scan rate: 50 mV/s.

DPVs of the Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene/GCE in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4). (A) containing 6 μM DA, 30 μM UA, 9 μM AC and different concentrations of AA (8–1080 μM); (B) containing 600 μM AA, 70 μM UA, 10 μM AC and different concentrations of DA (0.4–645 μM); (C) containing 300 μM AA, 17.5 μM DA, 17.5 μM AC and different concentrations of UA (0.9–1440 μM); (D) containing 280 μM AA, 8 μM DA, 20 μM UA and different concentrations of AC (0.6–482 μM).

DPVs and the calibration curves of simultaneous determination of AA, DA, UA and AC using Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene/GCE in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4). Concentration of four compounds: AA (8–2556 μM); DA (0.2–97.5 μM); UA (0.6–273 μM); AC (0.5–275 μM).
Illustration of the preparation of Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene/GCE.
The linear response ranges, linear regression equations and detection limits for AA, DA, UA, AC with the Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene/GCE (aindividual analysis, bsimultaneous analysis).
Comparisons of analytical performance of AA, DA, UA and AC on electrode modified with different materials.
Determination of AA, DA, UA and AC in human serum and urine samples.

head.ce:title: A quadruplet electrochemical platform for ultrasensitive and simultaneous detection of ascorbic acid, dopamine, uric acid and acetaminophen based on a ferrocene derivative functional Au NPs/carbon dots nanocomposite and graphene

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology & Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education, China), College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan Normal University, Changsha 410081, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology & Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education, China)
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a new nanomaterial of thiol functional ferrocene derivative (Fc-SH) stabilized Au NPs/carbon dots nanocomposite (Au/C NC) coupling with graphene modified glassy carbon electrode (Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene/GCE) was fabricated to serve as a quadruplet detection platform for ultrasensitive and simultaneous determination of ascorbic acid (AA), dopamine (DA), uric acid (UA) and acetaminophen (AC). The Au/C NC was synthesized by adding HAuCl4 into carbon nanodots solution without using any additional reductant and stabilizing agent. Then the Fc-SH was utilized as the protective and capping agent to modify the Au/C NC. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), UV–Vis, Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), cyclic voltammetry (CV), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were adopted to characterize the morphology and electrochemical properties of the materials and the electrodes. The Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene/GCE exhibits a synergistic catalytic and amplification effects towards oxidation of AA, DA, UA and AC owing to the existence of the nanomaterial and electron mediator. When simultaneous detection of AA, DA, UA and AC, the oxidation peak potentials of the four compounds on the electrode can be well separated and the peak currents were linearly dependent on their concentrations. The quadruplet detection platform shows excellent linear range and ultrasensitive response to the four components, the detection limits were estimated to be as low as 1.00, 0.05, 0.12 and 0.10 μM (S/N = 3), and the modified electrode exhibits excellent stability and reproducibility. The proposed electrode has been successfully applied to detect of these four analytes in real samples with satisfactory results.

ce:section.ce:para: Considering the small biomolecules usually coexist in biological matrixes and play vital roles in physiological function of organisms, the detection of physiologically-related species in biological samples has attracted considerable attention in recent years [1,2]. Until now, many analytical methods have been used for the simultaneous determination of two or three components of the physiologically-related species, including high performance liquid chromatography [3], capillary electrophoresis [4,5], chemiluminescence [6–8], spectrometry [9], and electrochemical sensors [10–12]. Most of these methods are complicated, time-consuming and usually require specialized instruments, except for electrochemical sensors. Owing to its simplicity, good stability, low cost, fast response, high sensitivity and selectivity, electrochemical method may be a good alternative to simultaneous detect multiple analytes. However, seldom paper focuses on quadruplet detection based on electrochemical methods owing to the poor selectivity.

Ascorbic acid (AA), dopamine (DA), uric acid (UA) and acetaminophen (AC) play important roles in physiological function. AA is not only very popular for its antioxidant properties, but also widely used for the prevention and treatment of the common cold, mental illnesses, infertility, cancers and AIDs [2,13,14]. DA is a natural neurotransmitter which plays important roles in control of central nervous system, cardiovascular, renal, and hormonal functions [1,15,16]. UA is also an important biomolecule existing in blood and urine and its level was related with several diseases, such as gout, hyperuricaemia, and Lesch-Nyhan syndrome [13,17,18]. AC is an antipyretic and analgesic drug which is widely used for relieving and moderating pain associated with headache, backache, arthritis and postoperative pain [15]. However, the simultaneous electrochemical determination of AA, DA, UA and AC in samples is hindered by mutual interferences because the oxidation potentials of them at conventional electrodes are so close that it is difficult to separate their voltammetric peaks [1]. To overcome these problems, it is essential to develop excellent electrode modified materials for determination without cross interferences in routine analysis.

There are several reports for simultaneous electrochemical determination of several analytes of them. Wang et al. fabricated a electrochemical sensor based on copper terephthalate metal-organic framework-graphene oxide nanocomposite Cu(tpa)-GO for simultaneous determination of DA and AC [19]. A sensor based on seven-hole carbon nanotube paste multi-electrode array was prepared for simultaneous electrochemical determination of AA and DA by Peng et al. [13]. M. Amal Raj and S. Abraham John fabricated reduced graphene oxide modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE) by self-assembly method to simultaneous detect AA, DA and UA [20]. Li et al. prepared a sensor based on multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWNTs) bridged mesocellular graphene foam (MGF) nanocomposite (MWNTs/MGF) modified glassy carbon for simultaneous determination of AA, DA, UA and tryptophan (TRP) [2]. On the basis of these works, we know that for simultaneous detection of multi-analytes, one of the key issues is to discriminate each electrochemical signal from others. Therefore, how to construct simple, sensitive and selective biosensors for simultaneous analysis of multi-component in complex systems is still a challenge for analytical researchers.

For sensitive and selective detection, it is essential to amplifying the signals and discriminate each electrochemical signal at the same time [21]. On the one hand, in order to improve the catalytic activity of electrode, various nanomaterials [22] such as metal-organic framework [19] and metal oxides [15] can be employed. Metal nanomaterials through increasing the effective surface area of the sensor and improving the catalytic properties of the electrodes, can provide a sensitive detection platform for electroactive species [23–30]. Among the metal nanomaterials, gold-based nanomaterials have been widely used in modifying electrode due to their high stability, high electrocatalytic activity, excellent conductivity and fast electron transfer property [23,25–30]. On the other hand, Au NPs based nanocomposite, such as Au/carbon nanocomposite have been developed. Ju et al. prepared a sensor based on Au nanoparticles (Au NPs) on nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (Au NPs-N-GQDs) which exhibit high sensitivity and selectivity for electrochemical detection of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) [25]. To our knowledge, carbon nanodots (CDs) are quasi-spherical nanoparticles and they have good properties such as solubility, desirable biocompatibility and nontoxicity [25,31–37]. Li et al. fabricated an ionic liquid-functionalized fluorescent carbon nanodots (IL-CDs) sensor which displayed favorable electrocatalysis toward the reduction of O2 and H2O2 [31]. C-dots can also act as an excellent electron acceptor and electron donor [37], and therefore they have promising potential to be oxidize or reduce agents. Hence, C-dots were used as the reductant for direct synthesis of Au NPs and obtaining the Au/C NC. The as synthesized Au/C NC showed good biocompatibility, excellent stability and catalytic activity [32]. Meanwhile, if the Au/C NC was functionalized by a electron mediator, the catalysis activity may be further enhanced [15]. Therefore, preparing Au/C NC based material as the modification film may be a good candidate for detection platform.

In this work, a novel electron mediator stabilized Au/C NC (Fc-S-Au/C NC) was firstly prepared and a quadruplet signal amplification platform was designed based on the Fc-S-Au/C NC and graphene. The Au/C NC was prepared by adding HAuCl4 into C-dot solution by simply mixing. Thiol functional ferrocene derivative can be used as an electron transfer mediator, catalyst or electrochemical probe, which can easily self-assembly on Au NPs or Au/C NC through the well-developed Au–S chemistry [15,31,38–41]. The obtained Fc-S-Au/C NC was mixed with graphene solution and cast on the GCE to construct Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene/GCE. It was testified that Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene/GCE exhibits favorable electrocatalysis towards AA, DA, UA and AC. The four compounds can be well distinguished by their different peak potentials and they can be simultaneously detected in the quaternary mixture. Furthermore, this as-proposed sensing strategy also revealed excellent stability and reproducibility.

Chloroauric acid (HAuCl4·4H2O) was obtained from Shanghai Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Ethylene glycol (C2H6O2) was purchased from Huihong Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Graphene was purchased from XFNANO Co., Ltd. (Nanjing, China). Phenylethynyl ferrocene thiolate (Fc-SAc) were synthesized by ourselves, and characterized carefully in our published work [15]. Concentrated sulfuric acid (H2SO4) and ethanol absolute (C2H5OH) were purchased from Tianjin Fuyu Fine Chemical Co., Ltd. AA, DA, UA and AC were obtained from Aldrich and used as received. Phosphate buffer solution (PBS) was prepared using 0.1 M KH2PO4, 0.1 M K2HPO4 and 0.1 M K2SO4, and the pH was adjusted with NaOH and H2SO4 solution. The PBS was purged with pure nitrogen for at least 15 min before use. 0.01 M stock solutions of DA, UA, AC and 0.1 M AA were freshly prepared with the PBS. All other reagents were of analytical grade and used as received. Milli-Q ultrapure water was used in all experiments. The experiments were performed at room temperature.

The field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) micrographs and energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) were obtained with SEM (Zeiss Sigma, Germany). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were collected on a JEM-1230 transmission electronic microscope (JEOL, Japan). UV–vis spectra were conducted on the UV-2450 spectra-photometer (Shimadzu, Japan). FT-IR spectra were collected on a pressed pellet with KBr in the transmission mode on a Nexus-670 Fourier transform-infrared spectrophotometer (Nicolet Instrument Co. USA). Electrochemical experiments including cyclic voltammetry (CV), differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were performed on a research electrochemical workstation of SP-150 (BioLogic Science Instruments, France) controlled by EC-Lab software. The conventional three-electrode system was employed with a modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE) as the working electrode, a carbon rod as counter electrode, a KCl-saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as reference electrode. The pH values were measured on a pH S–3C digital acidometer (PR China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Au/C NC and the modified electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: The hydroxyl-rich C-dots were synthesized by one-pot electrochemical carbonization of ethylene glycol [32]. Briefly, ethylene glycol and sodium hydroxide were mixed and used as the electrolytes, platinum sheets as the positive and the negative electrodes. The C-dots were synthesized by electrolyzing the electrolytes under 30 V (DC) for 40 min. After electrolysis, the electrolyte solution was neutralized with hydrochloric acid, and then dialyzed (Mw 1000) against water for two days. The supernatant was centrifugated to remove the residual components. The Au/C NC was prepared via adding 2.1 μL of 48.56 mM HAuCl4 into 1 mL of the C-dots solution by simply mixing and the color of the mixture changed from yellow to red immediately (within 30 s) at room temperature. The C-dots and Au/C NC were stored at 4 °C in refrigerator when not in use.

The newly phenylethynyl ferrocene thiolate (Fc-SAc) was synthesized and characterized according to our previous literature [15] and dissolved in ethanol and 5 μL of concentrated H2SO4 was added in for hydrolyzing. The solution was incubated for 4 h, and Fc-SAc would hydrolyze into phenylethynyl ferrocene thiol (Fc-SH). Then the solution was added into Au/C NC solution under stirring and the Fc-SH molecules self-assembled on the surface of Au/C NC for 24 h. The product was washed with ultrapure water and C2H5OH, and dried at 50 °C. After rinse, the Fc-S-Au/C NC was re-dispersed in water to form 0.1 mg/mL solution.

Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene modified GCE was prepared as described in Scheme 1 . Prior to modification, the bare GCE was polished to be a mirror-like shine with 50 nm alumina slurry, then washed successively with anhydrous alcohol and ultrapure water in an ultrasonic bath and dried in N2. Firstly, 1 mg graphene was dispersed in 1 mL ultrapure water and sonicated for 30 min to obtain 1 mg/mL graphene suspension. 100 μL of 2 mg/mL Fc-S-Au/C NC solution was mixed with equal volume of graphene solution. 10 μL Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene was cast on the clean GCE and dried in air to obtain the Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene/GCE. The modified electrode was dried under air and rinsed with ultrapure water prior to use.

CV and DPV measurements were carried out in a three-electrode cell in 0.1 M PBS. Before every measurement, the electrolytic solutions were purged with nitrogen for at least 15 min. CVs and DPVs were performed in the range of −0.2 to 0.6 V. EIS measurements were measured at −0.2 to 0.6 V in 2 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−. The electrochemical parameters for DPVs were: pulse width, 280 ms; pulse height, 2.5 mV; step height, 20.0 mV; step time, 500 ms.

Serum samples were purchased from the Hospital of Hunan Normal University. 200 μL serum sample was transferred into the electrochemical cell and diluted to 10 mL with 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4). The human urine samples (200 μL) were diluted to 2 mL with 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4). The contents of AA, DA, UA and AC were determined using the standard addition method. To ascertain the correctness of the results, the diluted samples mentioned above were spiked with certain amounts of AA, DA, UA and AC, and then measured under the optimal conditions.

The morphologies and microstructures of C-dots (A), Au/C NC (B), Fc-S-Au/C NC (C) and Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene (D) were investigated by TEM. Fig. 1 A shows that the C-dots are of low multiple dispersity with the diameters ranging in 2–4 nm. The HRTEM image of one nanoparticle (Fig. 1A, inset) shows a crystalline structure with a lattice spacing of 0.20 nm, which can be attributed to the (102) diffraction planes of graphitic (sp2) carbon [32]. C-dots were used as the reductant and stabilizing agent. After addition of AuCl4 −, AuCl4 − was reduced to Au NPs by C-dots. And the remaining C-dots can be used as stabilizing agent preventing the aggregation of Au NPs. Therefore, Au NPs showed good dispersion in Fig. 1B. The as-synthesized Au NPs is nearly spherical with diameters ranging in 12–14 nm. The clear lattice spacing of 0.24 nm on the HRTEM image (Fig. 1B, inset) was attributed to the (111) lattice space of metallic Au. In comparison with C-dots, Au/C NC surrounded by a layer of compounds which may prevent the aggregation of Au NPs. Fig. 1C is the TEM of Fc-S-Au/C NC, it has an average diameter of 12 nm and the crystalline structure with a lattice spacing of 0.23 nm, which may be attributable to the (111) lattice space of metallic Au. Moreover, the diameter remained almost unchanged and the dispersion was better than that in the absence of ferrocene derivative. This may result from that the thiol compounds of the ferrocene derivative can act as an excellent protective agent and stabilizer to improve the dispersibility. Fig. 1D is the TEM image of Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene. It can be seen that the Au NPs or Au/C NC were uniformly distributed on the surface of graphene which had irregularly crumpled and wrinkled sheet-like structures. Fig. 1E is the SEM picture of Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene. It shows an obvious conglomeration surface with high roughness, which may suggested the flake-like graphene sheets. Though Au and Fc-SH cannot be obviously seen in the surface, the EDS (Fig. 1F) further confirmed the presence of Fe, S and Au. Therefore, these results indicated the Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene was successfully modified on the electrode.

Fig. 2 A shows the typical UV–vis spectra of Fc-SAc, C-dots, Au/C NC, and Fc-S-Au/C NC. It shows the change of the C-dots before and after addition of AuCl4 −. The as-synthesized C-dots have a strong absorption peak at 258 nm, which is typically attributed to the π-π* transition [32]. The absorption spectrum of Au/C NC shows another narrow peak at 527 nm compared with C-dots (Fig. 2A), which matches well with the typical plasmon resonance absorption peak of Au NPs. For the Fc-SAc, the characteristic peaks were located at 210 nm, 267 nm and 309 nm. After hydrolyzing into Fc-SH, the characteristic peaks were located at 208 nm, 268 nm and 307 nm, which were not obviously changed compared with Fc-SAc (Fig. S1 in supporting information). After Fc-SH was immobilized onto the Au/C NC surface, the absorbance peaks of Fc-S-Au/C NC were 273 and 325 nm. Though the peak for Au NPs was not obvious, the intensity of absorbance was increased. It may indicate that Fc-SH was successfully modified on the Au/C NC surface via Au–S bond to form the sulfur-based layer. Thiols usually act as ligands and stabilizers in modifying of Au NPs, which results in more stable and compatible nanomaterials [15]. Therefore, this method may offer a new way to obtain electron mediator-stabilized Au/C NC.

The FT-IR spectroscopy was used to confirm the successful modification of Fc-SH on Au/C NC surface. Fig. 2B shows the FT-IR spectra of Fc-SAc, C-dots, Au/C NC and Fc-S-Au/C NC. The characteristic peak of hydroxyl groups (–OH) at 3428 cm−1 and carbonyl groups (CO) at 1700 cm−1 were observed in the spectrum of C-dots. Compared with C-dots, the relative intensity of hydroxyl groups (–OH) decreased and the intensity of carbonyl groups (CO, 1700 cm−1) increased after reaction with HAuCl4, indicating that hydroxyl groups (–OH) on the surface of C-dots may reduce HAuCl4 into Au NPs and convert itself into carbonyl groups (CO) after reaction. Compared with the IR spectra of C-dots and Au/C NC, the characteristic peak of alkyne (CC, 2210 cm−1) and ferrocene (833 cm−1) were observed on the Fc-SAc and Fc-S-Au/C NC. After Fc-SH was stably immobilized onto the Au/C NC surface through Au–S bonding, the relative intensity of carbonyl groups (CO, 1700 cm−1) and S–CO (1120 cm−1) decreased. Therefore, the above results clearly confirmed that Au/C NC was successfully modified with Fc-SH molecules.

The [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− couples are commonly utilized as electrochemical probe for the investigation of the properties of the sensors. The electrochemical performance of Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene/GCE was investigated in 2.0 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− redox probe solution. As Fig. 3 A shows, compared with those of the bare GCE, Au/C NC/GCE, graphene/GCE, Fc-S-Au/C NC/GCE, it could be seen that the redox peaks at the Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene were significantly enhanced. This may attributed to that the ferrocene derivatives are excellent electron mediators and graphene have large surface area and they can accelerate the electron transfer and result in larger current response. The peak-to-peak potential (ΔE p) at Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene/GCE between the anodic and cathodic peak potential is about 58 mV, while those at the bare GCE, Au/C NC, graphene and Fc-S-Au/C NC are 140, 106, 70 and 84 mV, respectively. Therefore, such a small ΔE p value at Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene/GCE reveals a fast and quasi-reversible electron-transfer process, indicating that this electrode can provide a favorable microenvironment to undergo facile electron-transfer reaction. Fig. S2 in the supporting information showed the cyclic voltammograms obtained from Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene/GCE and Fc-S-Au/graphene/GCE. From the comparison, it can be seen that the current at Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene/GCE was higher than that at Fc-S-Au/graphene/GCE, indicating the charge transfer process was relatively faster than the former. Hence, Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene/GCE may be a good electrochemical detection platform for electroactive species.

EIS is a suitable technique for investigation of the electrode surface dependent charge transfer process. Fig. 3B displays the EIS results of different electrodes which were fitted on the equivalent circuit as given in inset of Fig. 3B. This equivalent circuit includes the ohmic resistance of the electrolyte (R s), the Warburg impedance (Zw), the electron-transfer resistance (R ct) and an interfacial capacitance (C dl). The EIS included a semicircular part and a linear part. The semicircle diameter represents the electron-transfer resistance R ct, which dominates the electron transfer kinetics of the redox probe at the electrode interface. The linear part at lower frequency corresponded to the diffusion process. As Fig. 3B shows, the bare GCE displayed a large semicircle with a R ct of about 1444 Ω, which is due to low conductivity that led to poor electron transfer between the solution and electrode interface. When the bare electrode was modified with Au/C NC, the R ct decreased to 1029 Ω, indicating that the Au/C NC can accelerate the electron transfer. After Fc-SH was stably immobilized onto the Au/C NC surface and modified on the GCE, the R ct decreased to 447.5 Ω. The R ct value further decreased when the modification of Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene on electrode (7.1 Ω), which was lower than the R ct values obtained for bare GCE, Au/C NC/GCE, graphene/GCE, Fc-S-Au/C NC/GCE. It was reciprocal relationship between surface resistance and electrical conductivity. Therefore, the electrical conductivities of the electrodes were: GCE < Au/C NC/GCE < Fc-S-Au/C NC/GCE < graphene/GCE < Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene. This indicated that Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene can improve the electron transfer kinetics compared with graphene or Fc-S-Au/C NC. It also revealed the satisfactory electrical conductivity of Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene. The reason may be that Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene exhibits high electrical conductivity and large surface area that can effectively promote the electron transfer between the target molecule and the electrode. These results were identical with the results of CV. Moreover, the comparison of Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene and Fc-S-Au/graphene could be seen by EIS (Fig. S2 in supporting information), which exhibited that the R ct value of Fc-S-Au/graphene (28.8 Ω) was larger than that of Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene. Therefore, Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene possessed the minimum electron transfer resistance and showed the best conductivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical behaviors of AA, DA, UA and AC on modified electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 shows individual oxidation of AA, DA, UA and AC at different modified electrodes by CV in 0.1 M pH 7.4 PBS. As shown in Fig. 4, the peak currents on the graphene/GCE (d) and Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene/GCE (e) was improved compared with those on the bare GCE (a), Au/C NC (b) and Fc-S-Au/C NC (c). However, the peak currents on the Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene/GCE increased obviously comparing with those of graphene. The oxidation potentials of AA, DA, UA and AC are 0.05 V, 0.18 V, 0.31 V and 0.39 V at the Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene/GCE, respectively. Therefore, it is possible to determine the four constituents simultaneously on the Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene/GCE.

Fig. 5 shows CVs and DPVs of different modified electrodes in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4) containing the mixture of AA, DA, UA and AC. From Fig. 5A, it can be seen that, the voltammetric peaks of AA, DA, UA and AC overlapped severely on bare GCE (a), Au/C NC (b) and Fc-S-Au/C NC (c) showed two broad peaks with smaller peak current. Hence, it is difficult to separate the oxidation peaks of the four components individually. However, four well-defined oxidation peaks corresponding to AA, DA, UA and AC were observed in CVs when graphene/GCE and Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene/GCE were used. The oxidation peak currents of AA, DA, UA and AC were obviously enhanced and the peak-to-peak potential separation of UA-AC increased on the Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene/GCE.

DPV is a powerful electrochemical technique that is often used for quantitative analysis because of its higher sensitivity and better resolution than CV. From Fig. 5B, it can be seen that the peaks for the four analytes overlapped at the bare GCE (a), Au/C NC (b) and Fc-S-Au/C NC (c). When Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene and graphene were modified on GCE, the peak currents improved obviously and the peak potentials were well separated from each other. Especially at the Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene modified GCE, the oxidation peaks of the four components located at −0.06, 0.12, 0.24 and 0.34 V, respectively. Meanwhile, the peak potentials for AA and AC negatively and positively shifted respectively compared with those on the graphene/GCE. The peak separations for AA-DA, DA-UA and UA-AC were 180, 120, 100 mV, respectively. The peak separations and peak currents of them were increased compared with other cases, indicating that the simultaneous determination of the four species can be realized at Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene/GCE. The ferrocene derivative was not good at separating the four substances, but graphene could. Although graphene could separate the oxidation peaks of the four components, the amplification effect can be serverly enhanced in the presence of ferrocene derivative. Hence, graphene can separate the oxidation peaks of the four components, Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene could increase the effective area and amplify the signals to improve the response of peak current. Therefore, these results indicated that graphene and ferrocene derivatives are both equally important, both are indispensable.

The main reasons for signal amplification and discrimination are as follows: Firstly, nanomaterials exhibit high effective surface area which can improve the detection sensitivity. Secondly, graphene, an atomic sheet of sp2 bonded carbon atoms, possesses a high specific surface area, extraordinary electronic transport properties and high electrocatalytic activities [42–44]. The real surface area of Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene and graphene were tested by CV. According to the Randles–Sevcik equation (ip = kn3/2AD1/2 C v1/2), the peak current is linear with the square root of sweeping speed (See Fig. S3). Then, the effective area of Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene and graphene can be calculated according to the slope of the graph. The effective area of Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene and graphene were 0.1206 cm2 and 0.1145 cm2, respectively. The surface area of bare electrode was 0.071 cm2. It can be seen that the effective area of Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene was larger than graphene, indicating that Fc-S-Au/C NC could improve the effective area for the oxidation processes. Thirdly, ferrocene derivatives are excellent electron mediators and they can accelerate the electron transfer and result in larger current response. The CV of Fc-SAc in CH3CN (containing 0.1 M LiClO4) was shown in Fig. S4A (in the supporting information). The oxidation and reduction peaks appeared at 0.438 and 0.369 V, which may be attributed to the Fc+/Fc couples in Fc-SAc. The peaks were positively-shifted when comparing with ferrocene. Fc-SAc has relatively larger conjugation structure, and thus results in the difficulty in oxidation. Fig. S4B shows the CV of Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene/GCE and Au/C NC/graphene/GCE in PBS. The Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene/GCE shows a pair of peaks at 0.399/0.345 V, while the Au/C NC/graphene/GCE has no peak. Therefore, the above results clearly confirmed that the redox peaks were the characteristic redox peaks of Fc-SAc. Ferrocene has good thermal stability and tolerance toward oxygen due to the interaction between the iron atom and the cyclopentadienyl ring. Additionally, ferrocene has a lower oxidation potential to lose an electron on account of the oxidizability of its iron atom and two stable redox states. If they are properly designed, ferrocene derivatives and ferrocene-based polymers and dendrimers which have a fast electron-transfer rate and exhibit excellent charge/discharged properties can be applied in molecular recognition and as electrochemical sensors [40]. Therefore, ferrocene is a good electroactive compound and can be used as an electron transfer mediator. Hence, a signal amplification platform for ultrasensitive detection of the four compounds can be realized on the Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene/GCE owing to the specific properties of the nanomaterials and electron mediator.

In order to further investigate the amplifying effect, Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene and Fc-S-Au/graphene modified GCE were compared. Fig. S5 in the supporting information shows the CVs and DPVs of Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene and Fc-S-Au/graphene in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4) containing the mixture of AA, DA, UA and AC. It was found that the peak currents of the electroactive species at Fc-S-Au/graphene were obviously smaller than those at Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene/GCE. This meant that the component of Au/C NC and the C-dots in the composite also had a positive effect on improving the electrochemical responses of AA, DA, UA and AC.

To achieve prominent analytical performance of the fabricated biosensor, we investigated the effects of several experiment parameters including the volume ratio of Fc-S-Au/C NC to graphene, the amount of Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene, and pH value of the electrolyte solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of volume ratio of Fc-S-Au/C NC to graphene
ce:section.ce:para: In order to seek for the optimal volume ratio of Fc-S-Au/C NC to graphene, different volume ratio of Fc-S-Au/C NC to graphene was investigated. As shown in Fig. S6A, the peak current increases with the increase of the amount of Fc-S-Au/C NC. Until the volume ratio of Fc-S-Au/C NC to graphene was 1/1, the peak currents reached to its maximum, then decreased with the further increase of volume ratio. So the optimal volume ratio of Fc-S-Au/C NC to graphene was 1/1 and following experiments were carried out at this ratio.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of amount of Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene modified on GCE
ce:section.ce:para: The effects of the amount of Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene nanocomposite on the DPV responses of the modified GCE was examined in Fig. S6B. It can be seen that all peak currents of AA, DA, UA and AC increased gradually with increasing the amount of Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene from 4.0 μL to 10.0 μL until it reached a maximum at 10.0 μL, and then decreased gradually. Hence, 10 μL Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene was chosen as the best amount for modification.

The effect of pH of the detection solution was investigated by DPV in Fig. S6C. Since the pH of body fluid is around neutral, the neutral and neighboring pH (5, 6, 7.4, 8 and 9) were conducted to obtain best condition in this experiment. With the increase of pH value, all the peak potentials negatively-shifted, indicating that protons involved in the oxidation processes. It is clear from Fig. S6C that the current response increases with the increase in pH and reaches maximum at pH 7.4. And the peak current was no apparent change with further increase of the pH. Considering the determination sensitivity, PBS of 7.4 was chosen as the optimal experimental condition in the subsequent electrochemical detection, which was also in accordance with the pH of body fluid.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simultaneous detection of AA, DA, UA and AC
ce:section.ce:para: DPV was utilized to investigate the analytical performance of the sensor. Fig. 6 shows the DPV responses of the mixture of AA, DA, UA and AC, in which the concentration of one substance changed, while the other three species remained constant. Under the optimized conditions, their peak potentials were at −0.06 V, 0.12 V, 0.24 V and 0.34 V, respectively, and they kept almost unchanged with the changing concentrations of one analyte. Moreover, the peak currents of the detected substance increased linearly with the increase of its concentrations in wide range, as seen in Fig. 6. The detection limits of AA, DA, UA and AC for the individual detection of the four-components can be estimated by the signal/noise, given the value of 1.00, 0.08, 0.18 and 0.12 μM (S/N = 3), respectively. The detailed results are shown in Table 1 .

Simultaneous detection of AA, DA, UA and AC was carried out and the results are shown in Fig. 7 . As is well seen, four well-defined and resolved peaks were observed, suggesting that the Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene/GCE was appropriate for simultaneous determination of the four species as an electrochemical sensor. Under the optimal conditions, the peak currents for AA, DA, UA and AC increased with their concentrations. The peak potentials kept steady and the calibration curves were linear in certain ranges, as seen in Fig. 7. The results indicated that Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene/GCE possessed satisfactory linear ranges and detection limits for the simultaneous detection of AA, DA, UA and AC. The detection limits of AA, DA, UA and AC for the simultaneous detection of the four components are 1.00, 0.05, 0.12 and 0.10 μM (S/N = 3), respectively. The detailed results are shown in Table 1.

Table 2 summarized the comparison of analytical performances of the proposed electrode with other works. It could be deduced that the sensor of Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene/GCE presented in this work possessed satisfactory linear ranges and detection limits for the simultaneous detection of four analytes, which opens a new way for the simultaneous and sensitive determination of AA, DA, UA and AC.

The reproducibility and stability of the Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene/GCE were investigated. The stability of the prepared electrode was examined by using the same Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene/GCE for 5 repetitive measurements containing these four analytes. The relative standard deviation (RSD) for each substance was less than 6.5%, confirming that the modified electrode for determination of AA, DA, UA and AC was stable. The fabrication reproducibility was estimated by using four modified electrodes independently prepared by the same procedures, the RSD was less than 3.7%. The modified electrode was stored at room temperature when it was not in use and the response of the electrode remains 94%, 95%, 93%, 94% for AA, DA, UA and AC of its initial value after 3 weeks. These results indicated that the proposed sensor had excellent reproducibility and stability.

To evaluate the validity of the proposed method, the Fc-S-NC/graphene/GCE was applied for the simultaneous determination of AA, DA, UA and AC in serum and urine samples. In order to avoid the interferences of the real samples matrix and fit into the linear ranges of AA, DA, UA and AC, only diluted serum and urine samples were added into the electrochemical cell. The human serum and urine samples were diluted with PBS (0.10 M, pH 7.4) before detection. To ascertain the correctness of the results, real samples were analyzed by the standard addition method and the diluted samples mentioned above were spiked with certain amounts of AA, DA, UA and AC and then detected. The recovery of the spiked samples ranged from 94.0% to 104.0% (Table 3 ), indicating that the Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene/GCE was reliable and sensitive enough for the determination of AA, DA, UA and AC in real samples.

In summary, a novel and simple signal amplification platform based on an electron mediator stabilized Au/C nanocomposite and graphene was successfully constructed and employed for the simultaneous determination of AA, DA, UA and AC. The modified electrode exhibited high sensitivity and electrocatalytic activity towards the oxidation of AA, DA, UA and AC. This novel platform exhibited four well-defined redox peaks, which resolved the overlapped and interference of the four components. This was attributed to the large surface area, high conductivity, excellent catalytic activity and amplification effect of Fc-S-Au/C NC/graphene nanocomposite. This fabricated sensor also showed excellent stability, reproducibility and great potential application in the detection of AA, DA, UA and AC in serum and urine samples.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21305042, 21375037), Scientific Research Fund of Hunan Provincial Education Department (14B116), Science and Technology Department (14JJ4030, 13JJ2020), the Opening Fund of state key laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics (2013017), the construct program of the key discipline in Hunan province and the Aid Program for Science and Technology Innovative Research Team in Higher Educational Institutions of Hunan Province.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2015.11.021.
